_comment: Offline fallback story 2 of 3. Selected when LLM_FALLBACK_STORY=2. One coherent settlement -- Saltmarrow -- matching every generator's schema, so the offline pipeline reads as a single place. theme/biome from the caller override the settlement's theme/biome fields; the authored fiction stays otherwise.

settlement.name: Saltmarrow
settlement.era: the third generation since the north levee was raised
settlement.founding_story: Saltmarrow spread its white salt-pans across the tidal flats where a river met the sea, and grew rich drying brine under the wind. When the spring tides broke the old dyke and drowned the low fields, the survivors raised a greater levee of stone and packed earth, and hung a tide-bell at every jetty to be rung when the water climbs too fast.
settlement.collective_fear: the equinox tides breaking the levee again
settlement.historical_wound: the Drowning that took the low fields
settlement.worldview: the sea gives and the sea takes back
settlement.social_structure: a guild of pan-masters under the levee-warden

goal.summary: raise the north levee a course higher before the equinox tides
goal.stakes: one more Drowning and the salt-pans would be lost to the sea for good.

shared_events: the night the tide-bells all rang at once
the Drowning of the low fields
the ferryman's last crossing in the storm
the summer the pans yielded double salt
the equinox the levee held by a handspan

subtitles.town: where the salt-pans whiten under a steady wind
subtitles.nature: the tidal flats, counted by the moon
subtitles.ruins: the drowned low fields, still marked at low water
subtitles.landmark: the great north levee that turned the sea
subtitles.default: a tide-bell waits at every jetty

relics.name: The Levee-Warden's Tide Charm
relics.description: Worn by whoever answers for the north dyke.
relics.lore: It has hung at the levee gate since the night the tide-bells all rang at once.

relics.name: Old Marsh's Nautilus Horn
relics.description: Sounded to call the pan-hands off the flats.
relics.lore: Its low note carries further than any voice when the water starts to climb.

relics.name: The Drowning Tally
relics.description: Every field lost, every soul the sea gave back.
relics.lore: Kept since the Drowning so no name from the low fields is ever forgotten.

relics.name: Pan-Master's Seal of Saltmarrow
relics.description: Pressed into every sealed sack of guild salt.
relics.lore: Only a master of the panyards may carry it, and only one lives at a time.

relics.name: Widow Sella's Tide-Bell
relics.description: The first bell recast after the Drowning.
relics.lore: She rang it until her arms failed the night the levee held by a handspan.

relics.name: The First Salt-Brick
relics.description: Pressed from the very first pan to dry clean.
relics.lore: White as bone and hard as stone, it is passed hand to hand at the salt-blessing.

relics.name: Tern-Feather of the North Levee
relics.description: Dropped where the terns nest on the new dyke.
relics.lore: The pan-hands say a tern on the levee means the tide will be kind.

relics.name: The Ferryman's Lantern
relics.description: Carried on the last crossing before the storm.
relics.lore: It was found still burning, wedged in the reeds, three days after he was lost.

relics.name: Brined Compass of Old Marsh
relics.description: Salt-fogged but never once wrong on the flats.
relics.lore: Old Marsh reads the tide by it when the fog swallows the jetties whole.

relics.name: The Equinox Trident
relics.description: Set above the levee gate each equinox.
relics.lore: Raised to the climbing sea as a promise that Saltmarrow will not drown twice.

relics.name: Scrimshaw Scute
relics.description: Scrimmed with the map of the old low fields.
relics.lore: The lines mark acres now under water at every high tide.

relics.name: Pickled Marsh-Light
relics.description: Kept in brine to light the cistern-tunnels.
relics.lore: It glows the pale green of the flats at dusk, when the salt goes blue.

tools.name: Pan-Master Sella's Salt-Rake
tools.description: Draws the drying salt into ridges across the pan.
tools.lore: Worn thin at the edge from forty seasons of the same slow stroke.

tools.name: Old Marsh's Patient Rod
tools.description: Worked the tide-channels between the flats.
tools.lore: He fishes the ebb so the panyards have something hot at the turn of the tide.

tools.name: Drowned-Field Salvage Shovel
tools.description: Digs the old fields at the lowest water.
tools.lore: There is good black earth down there still, for those who read the tide right.

tools.name: The Levee Blessing-Oar
tools.description: Dipped in the sea at the yearly levee-blessing.
tools.lore: Gilded by the guild the season the pans yielded double salt.

tools.name: Cistern-Diver's Pick
tools.description: For clearing the tunnels beneath the dyke.
tools.lore: The only tool trusted below when the tide is pressing at the stone.

tools.name: Netmaker Doon's Shears
tools.description: Cuts and mends the drying-nets on the jetties.
tools.lore: Doon has re-hung every net in Brinegate at least twice.

tools.name: Saltern Tide-Hook
tools.description: Lifts the sluice-gates on the turning tide.
tools.lore: Timed wrong, it floods a week's salt; the pan-hands time it right.

diaries.page: Good wind three days running and the pans came up white and clean. If it holds to the equinox we will have salt enough to trade for the levee-stone. The sea gives, this week; I do not forget that it takes back.

Fished the ebb before dawn while the flats went blue with salt. The terns are nesting on the new levee, which the old hands call a kindly sign. I read the water by my brined compass when the fog will not lift.

Went out at the lowest water to dig the old low fields. There is black earth down there yet, and now and then a doorstep the Drowning did not carry off. I mend nets by day and salvage by moon, and ring my jetty-bell coming home.

Walked the whole north levee at first light, sounding the stone for soft courses. We must raise it a full course before the equinox tides -- the guild grumbles at the labour, but they were here the night it held by a handspan. The tide gives no second warning.

Down clearing the cistern-tunnels again, where the sea presses at the joints. Cold, black work, and you learn to trust your marsh-light over your own eyes. We shore what we can so the levee has dry footing when the water climbs.

Another day at the pans, raking salt with everyone who can stand. I keep a tide-bell by our door as my grandmother did, and count the moon like she taught me. We were drowned out once; we will not be caught sleeping twice.

Re-hung the drying-nets after the high wind took two down. The cord costs dear now that the low fields are gone to salt water, so I splice more than I cut. Doon says a mended net still catches fish, and he is right, as usual.

Worked a pan out on the very edge of the Drowned Acre today. Uncle Marl farmed that ground before the sea took it; now I dry salt where his barley stood. It is not a sad thing, only a turning. The sea gives and the sea takes back.

Kept the levee-gate bell through the spring tides, ringing the count as the water rose. It held, thank the old dyke and the new. I oil the clapper nightly so it will never fail on the night it is most needed.

The levee-tunnels take on more water each equinox, and we bail and shore and bail again. It is thankless below, but a wet foundation is a drowned town. I mark the high-water line on the stone each season and pray it does not climb.

Sella is teaching me to read the salt -- when it is ready, when the wind will spoil it, when to draw it in. My hands are cracked white with brine and I am proud of it. She says a good pan-master is only patience with a rake.

The terns came back to the levee earlier than last year, which Old Marsh calls a fair omen for the tides. I count their nests each morning and mark them so the salvagers keep clear. Where the birds trust the dyke, so may we.
